reviews.summary: This paper takes on the challenge of both playing a game as optimally as possible, while following instructions and learning from demonstrations (both of which may be sub-optimal). The paper formulates this problem as a joint optimization, and tackles it by alternating steps of RL training with steps of distilling both demonstrations and successful RL trajectories. The paper evaluates this approach on the MiniRTS game, and evaluates against a range of baselines on various aspects such as rate of winning under different command types, how humans rank the policy’s instruction following, etc.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Post-response update
-----------

The authors have addressed some of my main concerns, and more thoroughly evaluated their method. I believe these new experiments could be better integrated in to the main text, and the paper is still limited by experimenting only with a single environment, but I am increasing my score in accordance with the improvements.



Original review
--------------


Strengths:
* An interesting and well-motivated problem space and proposed methods.
* In-depth experiments evaluating different perspectives on the issues raised in one domain.
* Good ablations and baseline conditions.
* Generally well written and clear. 

Weaknesses:
*The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not directly supported, only through circumstantial evidence that (while helpful) is not as direct as it could be.
  - The fact that win rates decrease with random commands is very weak evidence that the commands are being followed. The fact that humans rank the RED policy slightly higher than the rest is a little better, but the effect is weak (as noted in the paper there is an even distribution over the first rank). The statistical significance of the effect is not quantified, but there would almost certainly not be a significant difference between RED and the Switch or Joint baselines. 
  - To address this concern, it would be ideal to actually evaluate how well the agent follows commands. For example, set up a situation like Fig. 1E, give the agent a command like “Retreat”, and then evaluate whether the agent actually moves its units away or simply continues attacking, and how long it maintains this behavior. This would be far stronger, direct evidence that the agent is actually following commands (rather than e.g. doing nothing, issuing resource gathering commands without actually retreating, etc.).
  - Performing these tests in *precisely* situations where the RL policy would do something very different would be the strongest test of behavior.
* Only evaluating on a single game makes it difficult to know whether the conclusions are generalizable; it would be ideal to perform experiments in 1-2 other settings as well.
* The general approach to the prior literature could be improved. For example:
  - The paper argues that “BC and IRL algorithms assume that the demonstrations are optimal”—however, a variety of prior works have addressed this problem in various ways, both from the perspective of IRL (e.g. https://proceedings.mlr.press/v97/brown19a.html) and from BC-like approaches conditioned on rewards (https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.02875), or just by using BC to initialize RL training (e.g., https://www.nature.com/articles/nature16961). While not all these perspectives would necessarily address the issue of also following commands, they are not incompatible with it, and the paper should discuss how such approaches might relate to (or complement) the proposed technique.
  - Relatedly, prior work on agents that follow human instructions (particularly https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.05672) has relied on a combination of BC, RL, and IRL to achieve better performance than BC alone; this would be useful to discuss.
* It seems to me that there is a fundamental tension between the goals of strictly following human commands, and winning the game. The proposed approach appears to pursue a policy that effectively tries to distort all human commands towards its own objective (i.e., to follow the command in the way that is most likely to lead to winning, as in Fig. 1C vs. 1D). Phrased in this way, it seems that there are clear alignment concerns about this approach. These should be discussed as a tradeoff/risk.
  - These concerns relate to the above questions about how well the agent is following commands—if the goal is for the agent to actually strictly follow human commands, it should not e.g. follow a retreat order for one second and then go right back to attacking.
  - It seems to me that it would be *far* safer to have a system that follows human commands first and foremost—i.e., a system that is “interruptable” (https://deepmindsafetyresearch.medium.com/building-safe-artificial-intelligence-52f5f75058f1 and https://www.ijcai.org/Proceedings/2017/0032.pdf)
  - This concern is *somewhat* ameliorated by the fact that the win rates under sub-optimal commands are much lower, but it depends on how frequently the commands are issued in these conditions.
  - Regardless, these issues should be discussed.
reviews.questions:
See above, but briefly:
* Could the authors demonstrate more directly how well the agents follow commands—particularly in cases where they commands are strongly divergent from the winning policy?
* What do the authors think might be the risks of the proposed algorithm?
reviews.limitations:
On the whole, I think this paper could be a useful contribution. The most important changes from my perspective would be (see above for full details):
* The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not particularly well supported. Experiments on how well the agents are actually following commands would be most important for improving my rating.
* There are risks to trying to minimally follow commands while pursuing another objective. Discussing these shouldn't be a major change.

Other limitations that I don't think are as critical to address, but would be helpful:
* Only evaluating on a single game makes it difficult to know how general the advantage is.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper takes on the challenge of both playing a game as optimally as possible, while following instructions and learning from demonstrations (both of which may be sub-optimal). The paper formulates this problem as a joint optimization, and tackles it by alternating steps of RL training with steps of distilling both demonstrations and successful RL trajectories. The paper evaluates this approach on the MiniRTS game, and evaluates against a range of baselines on various aspects such as rate of winning under different command types, how humans rank the policy’s instruction following, etc.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Post-response update
-----------

The authors have addressed some of my main concerns, and more thoroughly evaluated their method. I believe these new experiments could be better integrated in to the main text, and the paper is still limited by experimenting only with a single environment, but I am increasing my score in accordance with the improvements.



Original review
--------------


Strengths:
* An interesting and well-motivated problem space and proposed methods.
* In-depth experiments evaluating different perspectives on the issues raised in one domain.
* Good ablations and baseline conditions.
* Generally well written and clear. 

Weaknesses:
*The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not directly supported, only through circumstantial evidence that (while helpful) is not as direct as it could be.
  - The fact that win rates decrease with random commands is very weak evidence that the commands are being followed. The fact that humans rank the RED policy slightly higher than the rest is a little better, but the effect is weak (as noted in the paper there is an even distribution over the first rank). The statistical significance of the effect is not quantified, but there would almost certainly not be a significant difference between RED and the Switch or Joint baselines. 
  - To address this concern, it would be ideal to actually evaluate how well the agent follows commands. For example, set up a situation like Fig. 1E, give the agent a command like “Retreat”, and then evaluate whether the agent actually moves its units away or simply continues attacking, and how long it maintains this behavior. This would be far stronger, direct evidence that the agent is actually following commands (rather than e.g. doing nothing, issuing resource gathering commands without actually retreating, etc.).
  - Performing these tests in *precisely* situations where the RL policy would do something very different would be the strongest test of behavior.
* Only evaluating on a single game makes it difficult to know whether the conclusions are generalizable; it would be ideal to perform experiments in 1-2 other settings as well.
* The general approach to the prior literature could be improved. For example:
  - The paper argues that “BC and IRL algorithms assume that the demonstrations are optimal”—however, a variety of prior works have addressed this problem in various ways, both from the perspective of IRL (e.g. https://proceedings.mlr.press/v97/brown19a.html) and from BC-like approaches conditioned on rewards (https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.02875), or just by using BC to initialize RL training (e.g., https://www.nature.com/articles/nature16961). While not all these perspectives would necessarily address the issue of also following commands, they are not incompatible with it, and the paper should discuss how such approaches might relate to (or complement) the proposed technique.
  - Relatedly, prior work on agents that follow human instructions (particularly https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.05672) has relied on a combination of BC, RL, and IRL to achieve better performance than BC alone; this would be useful to discuss.
* It seems to me that there is a fundamental tension between the goals of strictly following human commands, and winning the game. The proposed approach appears to pursue a policy that effectively tries to distort all human commands towards its own objective (i.e., to follow the command in the way that is most likely to lead to winning, as in Fig. 1C vs. 1D). Phrased in this way, it seems that there are clear alignment concerns about this approach. These should be discussed as a tradeoff/risk.
  - These concerns relate to the above questions about how well the agent is following commands—if the goal is for the agent to actually strictly follow human commands, it should not e.g. follow a retreat order for one second and then go right back to attacking.
  - It seems to me that it would be *far* safer to have a system that follows human commands first and foremost—i.e., a system that is “interruptable” (https://deepmindsafetyresearch.medium.com/building-safe-artificial-intelligence-52f5f75058f1 and https://www.ijcai.org/Proceedings/2017/0032.pdf)
  - This concern is *somewhat* ameliorated by the fact that the win rates under sub-optimal commands are much lower, but it depends on how frequently the commands are issued in these conditions.
  - Regardless, these issues should be discussed.

Questions:
See above, but briefly:
* Could the authors demonstrate more directly how well the agents follow commands—particularly in cases where they commands are strongly divergent from the winning policy?
* What do the authors think might be the risks of the proposed algorithm?

Limitations:
On the whole, I think this paper could be a useful contribution. The most important changes from my perspective would be (see above for full details):
* The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not particularly well supported. Experiments on how well the agents are actually following commands would be most important for improving my rating.
* There are risks to trying to minimally follow commands while pursuing another objective. Discussing these shouldn't be a major change.

Other limitations that I don't think are as critical to address, but would be helpful:
* Only evaluating on a single game makes it difficult to know how general the advantage is.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

This paper introduces REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), an iterative reinforcement learning algorithm designed to address the challenge of Grounded Reinforcement Learning (GRL). GRL requires an agent to not only optimize game performance but also strictly adhere to human language commands and demonstrations, even when these commands are sub-optimal. The proposed RED algorithm alternates between traditional RL training and a distillation process that incorporates both human and successful self-generated trajectories. The authors evaluate RED's effectiveness in the MiniRTS game, measuring win rates, command-following metrics, and conducting human studies.

**Overall Assessment:**
The paper tackles an interesting and well-motivated problem in GRL, proposing a novel approach with thorough experimental evaluation in its chosen domain. While the work presents several strengths, it also exhibits significant weaknesses regarding the direct evidence for command-following, the generalizability of findings, the comprehensiveness of the literature review, and a critical discussion of the inherent tension between strict command following and optimal task completion. Despite these concerns, the core methodology and the initial experimental insights warrant a "Weak Accept" decision, with a strong recommendation for the authors to address the identified issues to significantly enhance the paper's impact and rigor.

**Strengths:**
*   The problem space of Grounded Reinforcement Learning, particularly the challenge of balancing optimal performance with strict adherence to potentially sub-optimal human commands, is highly relevant and well-motivated. The proposed RED algorithm offers an interesting approach to this complex challenge.
*   The experimental evaluation conducted on the MiniRTS game is in-depth, exploring various perspectives on the issues raised by the problem.
*   The paper includes good ablations and baseline conditions, which aid in understanding the contribution of different components of the RED algorithm.
*   The manuscript is generally well-written and clear, making the proposed methods and experimental setup understandable.

**Weaknesses:**
*   A primary concern is the lack of direct and statistically significant evidence to support the central claim that the learned policy is genuinely "grounded" in and "follows" human commands. The evidence presented often relies on circumstantial observations. For instance, a decrease in win rates when random commands are introduced is considered very weak evidence for command following. Similarly, while human ranking slightly favored RED for command-following, the effect is weak, lacks quantified statistical significance, and shows an even distribution of first-rank votes across policies. The paper currently lacks robust, direct proof that the agent is obeying commands rather than merely exhibiting behavior correlated with their presence.
*   The evaluation is limited to a single game environment, MiniRTS. This raises concerns about the generalizability of the conclusions drawn from the experiments, making it difficult to ascertain if the RED algorithm's effectiveness extends to other domains or types of tasks.
*   The discussion of prior literature is insufficient in several key areas. Specifically, the paper does not adequately cover existing work on learning from non-optimal demonstrations (e.g., certain Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) or Behavior Cloning (BC)-like approaches). Furthermore, there is a need for a more comprehensive review of prior research on agents that combine BC, RL, and IRL for instruction following, such as the work presented in https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.05672. Relevant works on non-optimal demonstrations like https://proceedings.mlr.press/v97/brown19a.html, https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.02875, and https://www.nature.com/articles/nature16961 should also be discussed.
*   There is a fundamental tension and unaddressed alignment concern between strictly following human commands (which may be sub-optimal) and optimizing for winning the game. The paper does not adequately discuss the potential risks and trade-offs inherent in this conflict. The proposed approach appears to subtly distort human commands towards its own objective (winning), which raises significant ethical considerations about agent obedience, safety, and the potential for agents to disregard human intent for self-optimized outcomes.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**
To address the aforementioned weaknesses and enhance the paper's overall impact, I strongly recommend the following:
1.  **Direct Command-Following Evaluation:** Implement more direct evaluations of command-following, especially in scenarios where commands strongly diverge from the winning policy. For example, setting up situations similar to Fig. 1E ("Retreat" command) and observing if units genuinely move away or continue attacking, particularly when such actions are counter to winning strategies. This would provide stronger evidence for the agent's ability to truly follow instructions.
2.  **Generalizability Assessment:** Perform experiments in at least one or two other game or task environments beyond MiniRTS. This would significantly strengthen the claims regarding the generalizability of the RED algorithm.
3.  **Comprehensive Literature Review:** Improve the discussion of prior work, specifically including more detailed coverage of methods that address learning from non-optimal demonstrations (e.g., specific IRL and BC-like approaches) and approaches that combine BC, RL, and IRL for instruction following. Concrete examples of relevant works have been provided in the weaknesses section.
4.  **Discussion of Alignment and Ethical Considerations:** Dedicate a section to discuss the potential risks, trade-offs, and alignment concerns related to the fundamental tension between strict command following and optimal task completion. This should include a discussion of ethical implications, the concept of "interruptible" systems (referencing works like https://deepmindsafetyresearch.medium.com/building-safe-artificial-intelligence-52f5f75058f1 and https://www.ijcai.org/Proceedings/2017/0032.pdf), and how the agent's objective function might unintentionally lead to misaligned behavior.

**Conclusion:**
This paper presents a valuable exploration into Grounded Reinforcement Learning with a well-motivated problem and an interesting algorithmic proposal. The experimental work is thorough within its scope, and the paper is generally well-written. However, the core claims regarding command-following require more direct and statistically robust evidence. Furthermore, expanding the evaluation to multiple environments and strengthening the literature review and discussion of ethical and alignment concerns would significantly bolster the paper's contributions. Given the promising nature of the problem and the proposed method, I recommend a "Weak Accept" in its current form, with the expectation that the authors will diligently address the suggested improvements for the final version.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper takes on the challenge of both playing a game as optimally as possible, while following instructions and learning from demonstrations (both of which may be sub-optimal). The paper formulates this problem as a joint optimization, and tackles it by alternating steps of RL training with steps of distilling both demonstrations and successful RL trajectories. The paper evaluates this approach on the MiniRTS game, and evaluates against a range of baselines on various aspects such as rate of winning under different command types, how humans rank the policy’s instruction following, etc.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Post-response update
-----------

The authors have addressed some of my main concerns, and more thoroughly evaluated their method. I believe these new experiments could be better integrated in to the main text, and the paper is still limited by experimenting only with a single environment, but I am increasing my score in accordance with the improvements.



Original review
--------------


Strengths:
* An interesting and well-motivated problem space and proposed methods.
* In-depth experiments evaluating different perspectives on the issues raised in one domain.
* Good ablations and baseline conditions.
* Generally well written and clear. 

Weaknesses:
*The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not directly supported, only through circumstantial evidence that (while helpful) is not as direct as it could be.
  - The fact that win rates decrease with random commands is very weak evidence that the commands are being followed. The fact that humans rank the RED policy slightly higher than the rest is a little better, but the effect is weak (as noted in the paper there is an even distribution over the first rank). The statistical significance of the effect is not quantified, but there would almost certainly not be a significant difference between RED and the Switch or Joint baselines. 
  - To address this concern, it would be ideal to actually evaluate how well the agent follows commands. For example, set up a situation like Fig. 1E, give the agent a command like “Retreat”, and then evaluate whether the agent actually moves its units away or simply continues attacking, and how long it maintains this behavior. This would be far stronger, direct evidence that the agent is actually following commands (rather than e.g. doing nothing, issuing resource gathering commands without actually retreating, etc.).
  - Performing these tests in *precisely* situations where the RL policy would do something very different would be the strongest test of behavior.
* Only evaluating on a single game makes it difficult to know whether the conclusions are generalizable; it would be ideal to perform experiments in 1-2 other settings as well.
* The general approach to the prior literature could be improved. For example:
  - The paper argues that “BC and IRL algorithms assume that the demonstrations are optimal”—however, a variety of prior works have addressed this problem in various ways, both from the perspective of IRL (e.g. https://proceedings.mlr.press/v97/brown19a.html) and from BC-like approaches conditioned on rewards (https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.02875), or just by using BC to initialize RL training (e.g., https://www.nature.com/articles/nature16961). While not all these perspectives would necessarily address the issue of also following commands, they are not incompatible with it, and the paper should discuss how such approaches might relate to (or complement) the proposed technique.
  - Relatedly, prior work on agents that follow human instructions (particularly https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.05672) has relied on a combination of BC, RL, and IRL to achieve better performance than BC alone; this would be useful to discuss.
* It seems to me that there is a fundamental tension between the goals of strictly following human commands, and winning the game. The proposed approach appears to pursue a policy that effectively tries to distort all human commands towards its own objective (i.e., to follow the command in the way that is most likely to lead to winning, as in Fig. 1C vs. 1D). Phrased in this way, it seems that there are clear alignment concerns about this approach. These should be discussed as a tradeoff/risk.
  - These concerns relate to the above questions about how well the agent is following commands—if the goal is for the agent to actually strictly follow human commands, it should not e.g. follow a retreat order for one second and then go right back to attacking.
  - It seems to me that it would be *far* safer to have a system that follows human commands first and foremost—i.e., a system that is “interruptable” (https://deepmindsafetyresearch.medium.com/building-safe-artificial-intelligence-52f5f75058f1 and https://www.ijcai.org/Proceedings/2017/0032.pdf)
  - This concern is *somewhat* ameliorated by the fact that the win rates under sub-optimal commands are much lower, but it depends on how frequently the commands are issued in these conditions.
  - Regardless, these issues should be discussed.

Questions:
See above, but briefly:
* Could the authors demonstrate more directly how well the agents follow commands—particularly in cases where they commands are strongly divergent from the winning policy?
* What do the authors think might be the risks of the proposed algorithm?

Limitations:
On the whole, I think this paper could be a useful contribution. The most important changes from my perspective would be (see above for full details):
* The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not particularly well supported. Experiments on how well the agents are actually following commands would be most important for improving my rating.
* There are risks to trying to minimally follow commands while pursuing another objective. Discussing these shouldn't be a major change.

Other limitations that I don't think are as critical to address, but would be helpful:
* Only evaluating on a single game makes it difficult to know how general the advantage is.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
This paper studies Grounded RL, where an agent is trained to obey natural language commands while still trying to attain the highest reward. The paper proposed to iterate between a pure RL loss and a behavior cloning loss, where the BC loss takes trajectories from both human demo and from the winning ones in the current on-policy sample queue.

The method is evaluated on the MiniRTS env and dataset and it is shown that the method can balance between high winning rate when no command is given, and low winning rate when the command is random.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
* I appreciate the discussion around why the Lagrange multiplier is not suitable.
* The “random commands” is a good sanity check to see if the agent is affected by bad commands. 
* The assumptions and the alternatives are clearly stated (e.g. l151-152, l243-244, etc.)

Weaknesses:
* Although often observed in practice, as mentioned in l204-206, the fact that the policy can generalize at all beyond BC for any not-NA command relies on the architecture of the policy network. If that is the core, then this paper did not study the condition for which such generalization occurs. And it’s such a core assumption of the proposed method, that to leave it understudied makes me a bit uneasy.
* While I appreciate the social impact section, “should not result in any negative social impact” is a bit too extreme. One can easily imagine military uses where humans provide data in a simulator and the policy is later deployed in the real world. A stronger command-following RL agent will have a negative impact there, allowing better performance compared to an RL agent trained from scratch (which doesn’t work in the MiniRTS case). You may be able to argue that it has no worse social impact than say BC or other RL methods, but saying “any” is too strong.
reviews.questions:
* Did you run any experiments to see whether the agent is able to follow certain commands with clear goals? E.g. “build two archers” is a clearly defined one and easy to evaluate if supported by the MiniRTS api. 
* How did the “switch” policy do in terms of obeying the commands, especially the not-NA commands? It would be good to compare it with that of RED, to show that both can obey commands, but RED wins more – which is a central goal you are trying to achieve, iiuc.

Comments: 
* It will be interesting to see an “adversarial oracle” where they always choose the worst dominating units to build. 
* It is interesting that “human proxy” almost always performs worse than random.
reviews.limitations: The ratio between ||D_k|| and ||D|| and the threshold delta are dataset/env specific hparams which require extra evaluation to tune, and which will affect the command-obeying ability.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies Grounded RL, where an agent is trained to obey natural language commands while still trying to attain the highest reward. The paper proposed to iterate between a pure RL loss and a behavior cloning loss, where the BC loss takes trajectories from both human demo and from the winning ones in the current on-policy sample queue.

The method is evaluated on the MiniRTS env and dataset and it is shown that the method can balance between high winning rate when no command is given, and low winning rate when the command is random.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
* I appreciate the discussion around why the Lagrange multiplier is not suitable.
* The “random commands” is a good sanity check to see if the agent is affected by bad commands. 
* The assumptions and the alternatives are clearly stated (e.g. l151-152, l243-244, etc.)

Weaknesses:
* Although often observed in practice, as mentioned in l204-206, the fact that the policy can generalize at all beyond BC for any not-NA command relies on the architecture of the policy network. If that is the core, then this paper did not study the condition for which such generalization occurs. And it’s such a core assumption of the proposed method, that to leave it understudied makes me a bit uneasy.
* While I appreciate the social impact section, “should not result in any negative social impact” is a bit too extreme. One can easily imagine military uses where humans provide data in a simulator and the policy is later deployed in the real world. A stronger command-following RL agent will have a negative impact there, allowing better performance compared to an RL agent trained from scratch (which doesn’t work in the MiniRTS case). You may be able to argue that it has no worse social impact than say BC or other RL methods, but saying “any” is too strong.

Questions:
* Did you run any experiments to see whether the agent is able to follow certain commands with clear goals? E.g. “build two archers” is a clearly defined one and easy to evaluate if supported by the MiniRTS api. 
* How did the “switch” policy do in terms of obeying the commands, especially the not-NA commands? It would be good to compare it with that of RED, to show that both can obey commands, but RED wins more – which is a central goal you are trying to achieve, iiuc.

Comments: 
* It will be interesting to see an “adversarial oracle” where they always choose the worst dominating units to build. 
* It is interesting that “human proxy” almost always performs worse than random.

Limitations:
The ratio between ||D_k|| and ||D|| and the threshold delta are dataset/env specific hparams which require extra evaluation to tune, and which will affect the command-obeying ability.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

**Paper Summary:**

This paper introduces Grounded RL (GRL), a novel approach designed to train an agent capable of achieving high rewards while strictly adhering to natural language commands. The core of their method, REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), iteratively combines a pure RL loss with a behavior cloning loss. This is achieved by leveraging both human demonstrations and successful on-policy trajectories. The authors evaluate RED within the MiniRTS environment, showcasing its ability to effectively balance a high win rate with robust command-following capabilities, even in scenarios where commands might be suboptimal.

**Strengths:**

The paper presents several commendable aspects. The discussion regarding the unsuitability of the Lagrange multiplier approach for their problem formulation is well-articulated and appreciated, providing valuable context for their design choices. The inclusion of "random commands" as a baseline serves as an excellent sanity check, effectively demonstrating how the agent's performance is affected by poorly formed or irrelevant commands. Furthermore, the authors are clear in stating their assumptions and discussing alternative approaches, which contributes to the overall clarity and transparency of the work.

**Weaknesses:**

While the proposed method shows promise, there are two primary weaknesses that warrant attention. Firstly, a significant concern is the insufficient study of the conditions under which the policy network generalizes beyond behavior cloning for non-"NA" commands. This generalization is a core assumption of the proposed method, yet it remains empirically observed but theoretically and experimentally understudied in the current submission. Understanding this aspect is critical for the broader applicability and robustness of GRL. Secondly, the claim that the work "should not result in any negative social impact" is overly definitive and potentially extreme. Considering the nature of controlling agents with natural language commands, potential military or surveillance applications could lead to negative consequences, and this statement should be qualified to reflect such possibilities.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

To strengthen the paper, I offer several suggestions:

1.  **Generalization Study:** The generalization of the policy network beyond behavior cloning for diverse commands is a critical, yet empirically observed and theoretically understudied, assumption. Further experiments are needed to probe this aspect more deeply.
2.  **Hyperparameter Sensitivity:** The ratio between the size of on-policy winning trajectories (||D_k||) and human demonstrations (||D||), along with the constraint threshold delta, are identified as critical hyperparameters. These are specific to the dataset and environment and significantly influence the command-obeying ability. A more thorough analysis or discussion of their tuning and sensitivity would be beneficial.
3.  **Specific Goal Commands:** Conduct experiments to evaluate the agent's ability to follow commands with clear, specific goals, such as "build two archers." This would provide a more direct assessment of command adherence beyond general "win" or "don't care" commands.
4.  **Baseline Comparison:** Provide a more explicit comparison of the "Switch" policy's command-following ability with RED, especially for non-"NA" commands. This would help to more clearly emphasize RED's superior win rate while maintaining strong obedience.
5.  **Adversarial Oracle Experiment:** Consider implementing an "adversarial oracle" experiment where the oracle issues commands to build the worst dominating units. This could provide an interesting stress test for the agent's command-following robustness under challenging conditions.
6.  **"Human Proxy" Commander:** It was observed that the "human proxy" commander, learned from human data, almost consistently performs worse than random commands in terms of win rate. This is an interesting finding that deserves further discussion regarding its implications for learning from suboptimal human data.
7.  **Social Impact Statement:** As mentioned in the weaknesses, the statement regarding negative social impact should be qualified to acknowledge potential adverse applications.

**Overall Assessment:**

This paper presents a technically solid approach with Grounded RL and the RED method, demonstrating a promising balance between achieving high rewards and strictly following natural language commands in the MiniRTS environment. The discussion on the Lagrange multiplier and the use of random commands are clear strengths. While the current evaluation demonstrates the method's effectiveness, the limited study into the generalization capabilities beyond behavior cloning for diverse commands is a notable gap. Additionally, the social impact statement needs to be more nuanced. Despite these areas for improvement, the core idea and its demonstrated capabilities are valuable contributions to the field. The reasons to accept this paper, particularly the novel approach to balancing reward and obedience, outweigh the identified weaknesses. Addressing the suggested improvements would significantly enhance the paper's impact and robustness.

**Decision:** 5: Borderline accept

The proposed method, REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), iteratively combines a pure RL loss with a behavior cloning loss, using both human demonstrations and winning on-policy trajectories. The evaluation on the MiniRTS environment demonstrates that RED effectively balances a high win rate with strong command-following capabilities, even when commands are suboptimal.

The use of "random commands" provides a good sanity check to assess if the agent is affected by poor commands.

Further experimental validation is suggested to directly assess the agent's adherence to clear-goal commands and to more clearly differentiate RED's performance from the "Switch" baseline in terms of command obedience and win rate.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the problem of building reinforcement learning agents constrained by human demonstrations in the real-time strategy game MiniRTS.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
**Strengths** The paper proposes an interesting approach, REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), to find RL agents that trade off following possibly suboptimal human commands with finding an optimal solution to the MiniRTS environment. I think this is an interesting setting, and the manuscript presents the problem and related work in a clear and concise manner. The baselines the authors selected are appropriate for the problem set up

**Weaknesses**
While the manuscript claims that MiniRTS is harder than following natural language instructions in some grid world navigation environments (e.g., [1]). For starters, the problem of training from natural language seems harder for two reasons. First, understanding natural language instructions seems a harder problem than the command setup proposed in the paper. Furthermore, the problem that this previous navigation work analyzes is usually under sparse rewards (success/fail) while in the setting proposed in the paper there are dense demonstrations that may alleviate the complexity of the task.

In terms of novelty, constraining RL with demonstrations as has been extensively studied in the literature [2,3] and the architectural challenges for solving the language-grounded task were introduced in MiniRTS [4].

[1] Chevalier-Boisvert et al. BabyAI: A Platform to Study the Sample Efficiency of Grounded Language Learning. 2018
[2] Yang et al. Accelerating Safe Reinforcement Learning with Constraint-mismatched Policies. 2020
[3] Goecks et al. Integrating Behavior Cloning and Reinforcement Learning for Improved Performance in Dense and Sparse Reward Environments
[4] Hu et al. Hierarchical Decision Making by Generating and Following Natural Language Instructions
reviews.questions:
1. In the human study, how did the author evaluate the level of skills of participants and how they determined when the participants were "familiar" (line 353) with the game?  
2. Could the authors clarify the difference between the framework GRL they proposed and existing language grounded approached? The manuscript would benefit from the authors addressing the difference of their setting with existing language grounded approaches. I do not believe that the complexity of MiniRTS as an environment is a sufficient argument to dismiss previous work.
reviews.limitations: The author address some limitations of their work and the setting discussed in the paper is interesting from its potential societal impact of having RL agent that trade-off following human commands with the optimality of the actions it executes.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the problem of building reinforcement learning agents constrained by human demonstrations in the real-time strategy game MiniRTS.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
**Strengths** The paper proposes an interesting approach, REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), to find RL agents that trade off following possibly suboptimal human commands with finding an optimal solution to the MiniRTS environment. I think this is an interesting setting, and the manuscript presents the problem and related work in a clear and concise manner. The baselines the authors selected are appropriate for the problem set up

**Weaknesses**
While the manuscript claims that MiniRTS is harder than following natural language instructions in some grid world navigation environments (e.g., [1]). For starters, the problem of training from natural language seems harder for two reasons. First, understanding natural language instructions seems a harder problem than the command setup proposed in the paper. Furthermore, the problem that this previous navigation work analyzes is usually under sparse rewards (success/fail) while in the setting proposed in the paper there are dense demonstrations that may alleviate the complexity of the task.

In terms of novelty, constraining RL with demonstrations as has been extensively studied in the literature [2,3] and the architectural challenges for solving the language-grounded task were introduced in MiniRTS [4].

[1] Chevalier-Boisvert et al. BabyAI: A Platform to Study the Sample Efficiency of Grounded Language Learning. 2018
[2] Yang et al. Accelerating Safe Reinforcement Learning with Constraint-mismatched Policies. 2020
[3] Goecks et al. Integrating Behavior Cloning and Reinforcement Learning for Improved Performance in Dense and Sparse Reward Environments
[4] Hu et al. Hierarchical Decision Making by Generating and Following Natural Language Instructions

Questions:
1. In the human study, how did the author evaluate the level of skills of participants and how they determined when the participants were "familiar" (line 353) with the game?  
2. Could the authors clarify the difference between the framework GRL they proposed and existing language grounded approached? The manuscript would benefit from the authors addressing the difference of their setting with existing language grounded approaches. I do not believe that the complexity of MiniRTS as an environment is a sufficient argument to dismiss previous work.

Limitations:
The author address some limitations of their work and the setting discussed in the paper is interesting from its potential societal impact of having RL agent that trade-off following human commands with the optimality of the actions it executes.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

This paper introduces REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), a novel approach for Grounded Reinforcement Learning (GRL) within the real-time strategy game MiniRTS. The core objective of RED is to enable RL agents to achieve high rewards while faithfully adhering to high-level language commands provided by humans, even when these commands might be suboptimal. The proposed method aims to find a trade-off between following human instructions and discovering optimal solutions in the environment.

### Strengths

The paper presents an interesting problem setting where RL agents must balance following potentially suboptimal human commands with seeking optimal solutions. The proposed REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED) approach for this trade-off is conceptually appealing. Furthermore, the manuscript does an excellent job of presenting the problem and related work in a clear and concise manner, making it accessible to readers. The baselines selected for comparison are also appropriate and relevant to the problem setup, providing a solid foundation for evaluation.

### Weaknesses

While the problem is interesting, several claims and aspects of the work require further justification. The manuscript asserts that the GRL problem in MiniRTS is harder than following natural language instructions in certain grid world navigation environments (e.g., [1] Chevalier-Boisvert et al., 2018). This claim is questionable, as understanding natural language instructions in previous navigation work often involves more complex parsing and grounding challenges than the command setup proposed here. Moreover, prior navigation work frequently analyzes problems under sparse reward regimes, whereas the presence of dense demonstrations in this paper's setting may significantly alleviate task complexity.

A substantial concern also lies in the novelty of constraining RL with demonstrations. This area has been extensively studied in the literature (e.g., [2] Yang et al., 2020; [3] Goecks et al., 2019), and the paper's contribution here needs to be more clearly delineated from existing work. Additionally, the architectural challenges for solving language-grounded tasks in MiniRTS have been previously introduced (e.g., [4] Hu et al., 2019), suggesting that some foundational aspects are not entirely new.

### Suggestions for Improvement

To strengthen the manuscript, I offer the following suggestions:

1.  **Human Study Methodology:** Please clarify the methodology used for the human study. Specifically, detail how the level of skill of participants was evaluated and how 'familiarity' with the game MiniRTS was determined. This will enhance the credibility and reproducibility of the human data.
2.  **Comparison to Existing GRL:** The paper needs to more thoroughly clarify the differences between the proposed GRL framework and existing language-grounded approaches. Simply stating that MiniRTS is a more complex environment is not a sufficient argument to dismiss or differentiate from previous work. A deeper analysis of the unique challenges addressed by RED compared to other GRL methods is necessary.

### Main Points and Overall Assessment

The paper introduces a compelling approach, RED, for GRL in MiniRTS, aiming to balance agent autonomy with human guidance. The clarity of the problem statement and the selection of baselines are positive attributes. However, the reviewer challenges the paper's claims regarding the comparative difficulty of the GRL problem in MiniRTS against other language grounding tasks, particularly given the potential for simpler command understanding and the availability of dense demonstrations. The novelty of constraining RL with demonstrations is also questioned, referencing existing research. Addressing the methodological details of the human study and providing a clearer distinction from other language grounding frameworks are crucial for the paper's impact.

While the concerns regarding novelty and comparative difficulty are significant, the core problem setting and the proposed RED approach are interesting and hold promise. With adequate revisions addressing the points raised, particularly by clarifying the methodological details of the human study and providing a more robust discussion distinguishing the proposed GRL framework from existing literature, the paper could make a valuable contribution.

**Decision: 5: Borderline accept**

Summary:
This paper studies the problem of building reinforcement learning agents constrained by human demonstrations in the real-time strategy game MiniRTS.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
**Strengths** The paper proposes an interesting approach, REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), to find RL agents that trade off following possibly suboptimal human commands with finding an optimal solution to the MiniRTS environment. I think this is an interesting setting, and the manuscript presents the problem and related work in a clear and concise manner. The baselines the authors selected are appropriate for the problem set up

**Weaknesses**
While the manuscript claims that MiniRTS is harder than following natural language instructions in some grid world navigation environments (e.g., [1]). For starters, the problem of training from natural language seems harder for two reasons. First, understanding natural language instructions seems a harder problem than the command setup proposed in the paper. Furthermore, the problem that this previous navigation work analyzes is usually under sparse rewards (success/fail) while in the setting proposed in the paper there are dense demonstrations that may alleviate the complexity of the task.

In terms of novelty, constraining RL with demonstrations as has been extensively studied in the literature [2,3] and the architectural challenges for solving the language-grounded task were introduced in MiniRTS [4].

[1] Chevalier-Boisvert et al. BabyAI: A Platform to Study the Sample Efficiency of Grounded Language Learning. 2018
[2] Yang et al. Accelerating Safe Reinforcement Learning with Constraint-mismatched Policies. 2020
[3] Goecks et al. Integrating Behavior Cloning and Reinforcement Learning for Improved Performance in Dense and Sparse Reward Environments
[4] Hu et al. Hierarchical Decision Making by Generating and Following Natural Language Instructions

Questions:
1. In the human study, how did the author evaluate the level of skills of participants and how they determined when the participants were "familiar" (line 353) with the game?  
2. Could the authors clarify the difference between the framework GRL they proposed and existing language grounded approached? The manuscript would benefit from the authors addressing the difference of their setting with existing language grounded approaches. I do not believe that the complexity of MiniRTS as an environment is a sufficient argument to dismiss previous work.

Limitations:
The author address some limitations of their work and the setting discussed in the paper is interesting from its potential societal impact of having RL agent that trade-off following human commands with the optimality of the actions it executes.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Boosting Barely Robust Learners: A New Perspective on Adversarial Robustness

reviews.summary:
This paper studies how to achieve adversarially robust learning with weak robust learners. The investigation follows a quite natural agenda and gives quite complete answers. The contributions are three folds.

First, at the conceptual level, the paper propose the key definition of barely robust learning, and point out the importance to look at a larger perturbation set $U^{-1}(U)$. 

Second, the paper gives an oracle efficient reduction from adversarially robust learning to barely robust learning. This shows that barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is sufficient for adversarial robust learning. This is by Theorem 1 and Algorithm 1.

Third, the paper shows that adversarial robust learning implies barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ (Theorem 4), but it can not be achieved if one only has barely robust learning with respect to $U$ (Theorem 6). This shows that barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is necessary for adversarially robust learning.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses: I like the flow of writing in this paper, especially the introduction section. Although this is a purely theoretical paper, the pace makes its proposed questions and main contributions easy to understand. In particular, when I read the introduction, the first question coming to mind is, isn't boosting easier if the robust parameter $\beta$ is larger than 1/2? This is answered immediately in the paragraph "Landscape of boosting robustness." The second question coming to mind is, is the assumption of robust realizability more restrictive than the traditional concept of realizability. And the authors give examples to answer this question in the related work part. I also like the  discussion section (Section 5), which answers my concerns about the agnostic setting and computation efficiency. This part also propose several open questions.
reviews.questions: I am confident that I understand the theme and conclusions of the paper. But I only go through the algorithm and proof part, so it's hard for me to evaluate the novelty of the algorithm and the theoretical analysis. I think the concept of barely robustness is quite essential, but is the analysis somewhat similar to existing routines after one discover this concept?
reviews.limitations:
The proposed algorithm in the paper is more useful when the robust parameter $\beta$ is small, since existing results could not provide theoretical guarantees in this regime. However, experiments may be needed if the authors want to advertise their algorithm to be better than existing methods that are directly based on canonical results and already work for $\beta>1/2$.

I think the paper should put more space and content to discuss when the perturbation set $U^{-1}(U)$ is much larger than $U$, and when they are similar. The current paper only uses the $\ell_p$ ball examples, where requiring robustness with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is very reasonable (it's just a ball with twice radius). However, as looking at this larger perturbation set is one of the major novelty of the paper, the author should carefully explain for what $U$ this requirement is strong (which means boosting robustness is not easy, according to the theorems in the paper), and for what $U$ this requirement is weak (which means boosting robustness is easy).
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies how to achieve adversarially robust learning with weak robust learners. The investigation follows a quite natural agenda and gives quite complete answers. The contributions are three folds.

First, at the conceptual level, the paper propose the key definition of barely robust learning, and point out the importance to look at a larger perturbation set $U^{-1}(U)$. 

Second, the paper gives an oracle efficient reduction from adversarially robust learning to barely robust learning. This shows that barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is sufficient for adversarial robust learning. This is by Theorem 1 and Algorithm 1.

Third, the paper shows that adversarial robust learning implies barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ (Theorem 4), but it can not be achieved if one only has barely robust learning with respect to $U$ (Theorem 6). This shows that barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is necessary for adversarially robust learning.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
I like the flow of writing in this paper, especially the introduction section. Although this is a purely theoretical paper, the pace makes its proposed questions and main contributions easy to understand. In particular, when I read the introduction, the first question coming to mind is, isn't boosting easier if the robust parameter $\beta$ is larger than 1/2? This is answered immediately in the paragraph "Landscape of boosting robustness." The second question coming to mind is, is the assumption of robust realizability more restrictive than the traditional concept of realizability. And the authors give examples to answer this question in the related work part. I also like the  discussion section (Section 5), which answers my concerns about the agnostic setting and computation efficiency. This part also propose several open questions.

Questions:
I am confident that I understand the theme and conclusions of the paper. But I only go through the algorithm and proof part, so it's hard for me to evaluate the novelty of the algorithm and the theoretical analysis. I think the concept of barely robustness is quite essential, but is the analysis somewhat similar to existing routines after one discover this concept?

Limitations:
The proposed algorithm in the paper is more useful when the robust parameter $\beta$ is small, since existing results could not provide theoretical guarantees in this regime. However, experiments may be needed if the authors want to advertise their algorithm to be better than existing methods that are directly based on canonical results and already work for $\beta>1/2$.

I think the paper should put more space and content to discuss when the perturbation set $U^{-1}(U)$ is much larger than $U$, and when they are similar. The current paper only uses the $\ell_p$ ball examples, where requiring robustness with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is very reasonable (it's just a ball with twice radius). However, as looking at this larger perturbation set is one of the major novelty of the paper, the author should carefully explain for what $U$ this requirement is strong (which means boosting robustness is not easy, according to the theorems in the paper), and for what $U$ this requirement is weak (which means boosting robustness is easy).

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
This paper presents a significant theoretical framework for achieving adversarially robust learning by leveraging weak robust learners. It introduces the novel concept of 'barely robust learning' with respect to an expanded perturbation set, $U^{-1}(U)$, and rigorously establishes both the sufficiency and necessity of this concept for achieving strongly robust learning. The work is primarily theoretical, aiming to provide comprehensive answers to fundamental questions surrounding the boosting of adversarial robustness.

**Strengths:**
The paper is exceptionally well-written, with a particularly strong and engaging introduction that guides the reader through the proposed questions and main contributions effortlessly, a commendable feat for a purely theoretical work. The authors demonstrate a proactive approach by promptly addressing common questions that might arise, such as the implications of a robust parameter $\beta$ greater than 1/2 and the perceived restrictiveness of robust realizability. Furthermore, Section 5, the discussion section, is robust and effective, competently addressing concerns regarding the agnostic setting and computational efficiency, while also thoughtfully proposing several pertinent open questions for future research. The core contributions include defining 'barely robust learning' with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$, providing an oracle-efficient reduction (Theorem 1 and Algorithm 1) demonstrating its sufficiency for adversarial robust learning, and proving its necessity (Theorem 4 and Theorem 6), thereby showing that barely robust learning with respect to $U$ alone is insufficient. The $\beta$-RoBoost algorithm (Algorithm 1) offers an oracle-efficient boosting mechanism, particularly relevant for the regime where the robustness parameter $\beta \le 1/2$.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:**
While the paper makes substantial theoretical contributions, a few areas could benefit from further elaboration to enhance clarity and broader applicability.
Firstly, the proposed algorithm's primary utility appears to be for scenarios where the robust parameter $\beta$ is small ($\beta \le 1/2$), as existing results already provide theoretical guarantees for $\beta > 1/2$. If the authors wish to highlight specific advantages over existing methods in the $\beta > 1/2$ regime, conducting experiments to demonstrate such superiority could be considered, although this is not a strict requirement for a theoretical paper.
Secondly, the paper would benefit from a more comprehensive discussion on the characteristics of the perturbation set $U$ and its inverse image $U^{-1}(U)$. Specifically, it is not always clear when $U^{-1}(U)$ is significantly larger than $U$ versus when they are similar. The current exposition primarily relies on $\ell_p$ ball examples for $U$, which simplifies the relationship (e.g., a ball with twice the radius). Expanding the discussion (perhaps in Section 5) to include more diverse scenarios where these sets behave differently would enrich the paper significantly.
Relatedly, a clearer explanation is needed to delineate which types of $U$ make the requirement of robustness with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ "strong" (implying boosting is not easy) versus "weak" (implying boosting is easy) in the context of boosting robustness. This would provide deeper insight into the practical implications of the 'barely robust learning' definition.
Finally, while the core conceptual framework is highly novel, the reviewer found it challenging to fully evaluate the novelty of the algorithm and theoretical analysis in relation to existing routines *after* the central concept of $U^{-1}(U)$ is introduced. Further clarification on this aspect could strengthen the paper.

**Conclusion and Recommendation:**
This paper presents a technically solid and highly impactful theoretical contribution to the field of adversarial robustness. The introduction of 'barely robust learning' with respect to the $U^{-1}(U)$ perturbation set is a novel and crucial concept, providing a complete theoretical foundation for understanding and boosting adversarial robustness with weak learners, particularly in the challenging $\beta \le 1/2$ regime. The proofs of sufficiency (Theorem 1, Algorithm 1) and necessity (Theorem 4, Theorem 6) are rigorous and well-articulated. Despite the minor suggestions for expanding the discussion on the properties of $U$ and its relation to $U^{-1}(U)$, these do not detract from the fundamental soundness and significance of the theoretical results. The paper is well-written, clear, and addresses important open questions in the field. I recommend this paper for acceptance.

**Rating:** 7: Accept

reviews.original_rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
This paper mainly shows that strongly robust learnable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ is equivalent to barely robust learable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\mathcal{U})$, bu giving an algorithm $\beta$-RoBoost to boost robustness of barely robust learners and an algorithm to return a barely robust classifier through calling the strongly robust learner.

This paper also shows that barely robust learable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ is not sufficient for strongly robust learnable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ by a counterexample. What's more, the paper also gives algorithms to boost robustness with unlabeled data.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- This paper shows a sufficient and necessary condition for strongly robust learning and gives a novelty method to boost robustness of barely robust learners. The theoretical results are quite solid.
- The results of this paper in significant since it provides another way to study the robustness.
- The paper gives some high-level strategies to help understanding the spirit in the algorithms and proofs.

Weaknesses:
- As discussed in the discussion part, verifying whether a classifier is robust on the perturbation set $\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\mathcal{U})$ is a key issue in the computational efficiency of the proposed algorithm, but **to the best of my knowledge, there is now no theoretiacl gurantee for precisely verifying robustness for a general perturbation set and a general hypothesis class**. As stated in [1], the popular method random smooth might fail in verifying the $\ell_\infty$ robustness for high-dimensional images. So the realizability of the proposed algorithm highly depends on the development of robust verification.

[1] Blum, Avrim, et al. "Random Smoothing Might be Unable to Certify $\ell_\infty$ Robustness for High-Dimensional Images." J. Mach. Learn. Res. 21 (2020): 211-1.
reviews.questions:
In the proof of Theorem 6, the paper uses a randomized learner which do not need any data as input, and proves that it is $(\frac{1}{2},0,\delta)$-barely robust learnable. And the paper proves that the expected value of the **robust risk** w.r.t. the random choice of $\tilde{y}$ is $\frac{1}{2}$, but not to be $\frac{1}{2}$ with probability at least $1 - \delta$ over the choice of samples as in the definition of the barely robust learner. Although we can use concentration inequality to get a confidence, but it is over the random choice of $\tilde{y}$, so there seems to be a mismatch in the definition of the barely robust learner and the proof of Theorem 6.

In Corollary 2, the paper claims it is a stronger boosting guarantee, I wonder in what sense is it stronger than Thoerem 1? Is it falls on better sample complexity, or falls on weaker requirement on the barely robust learner, or others else?
reviews.limitations: The authors does.

This paper also shows that barely robust learable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ is not sufficient for strongly robust learnable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ by a counterexample. What's more, the paper also gives algorithms to boost robustness with unlabeled data.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

- The results of this paper in significant since it provides another way to study the robustness.

Weaknesses:

In Corollary 2, the paper claims it is a stronger boosting guarantee, I wonder in what sense is it stronger than Thoerem 1? Is it falls on better sample complexity, or falls on weaker requirement on the barely robust learner, or others else?

reviews.text:
## Peer Review

**Decision:** 8: Strong Accept

This paper makes significant theoretical contributions to the field of robust learning, offering a fresh perspective on understanding and achieving adversarial robustness. The authors establish a novel theoretical equivalence between strongly robust learning (with respect to a perturbation set \\(\\mathcal{U}\\)) and barely robust learning (with respect to \\(\\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\\mathcal{U})\\)). This foundational result is complemented by the introduction of \\(\\beta\\)-RoBoost, a sophisticated algorithm designed to boost the robustness of barely robust learners. The paper also critically demonstrates, through a counterexample, that merely achieving barely robust learning with respect to \\(\\mathcal{U}\\) is not sufficient for strongly robust learning with respect to the same \\(\\mathcal{U}\\), thereby clarifying an important distinction. The theoretical results are solid and well-presented, making this a technically strong paper with high potential impact.

### Strengths

*   **Fundamental Theoretical Contributions:** The paper provides a crucial theoretical underpinning by establishing a sufficient and necessary condition for strongly robust learning. This qualitative and quantitative equivalence between strongly robust learning (w.r.t. \\(\\mathcal{U}\\)) and barely robust learning (w.r.t. \\(\\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\\mathcal{U})\\)) is a significant advancement.
*   **Novel Algorithmic Approach:** The introduction of the \\(\\beta\\)-RoBoost algorithm is a key strength, offering a concrete and novel method for boosting the adversarial robustness of barely robust learners.
*   **Rigorous Theoretical Results:** The paper is built upon solid theoretical foundations, with well-derived results that contribute meaningfully to the understanding of robust learning. The inclusion of high-level strategies greatly aids in comprehending the algorithms and their proofs.
*   **New Perspective on Robustness:** The work provides a fresh and insightful perspective on studying robustness, which could stimulate new research directions in the field.
*   **Clarity on Robustness Notions:** The paper clearly delineates the relationship between different forms of robustness, particularly by showing that barely robust learning w.r.t. \\(\\mathcal{U}\\) is not sufficient for strongly robust learning w.r.t. \\(\\mathcal{U}\\).

### Weaknesses

*   **Computational Efficiency Concerns:** A primary concern lies with the practical realizability and computational efficiency of the proposed \\(\\beta\\)-RoBoost algorithm. Its effectiveness is highly dependent on the ability to precisely verify robustness for a general perturbation set, specifically \\(\\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\\mathcal{U})\\), and for a given hypothesis class. Currently, there is a lack of theoretical guarantees for efficient and provable robustness verification in such general settings. For instance, methods like random smoothing, while popular, might fail to verify \\(\\ell_\\infty\\) robustness for high-dimensional images, as cited by Blum et al. [1]. This dependence on an unsolved problem presents a practical bottleneck for the algorithm's broader applicability.
*   **Proof Consistency in Theorem 6:** There appears to be a potential mismatch in the proof of Theorem 6 regarding the robust risk guarantee. The proof shows the robust risk as an expected value with respect to random choices of \\(\\tilde{y}\\) from a randomized learner that is \\((\\frac{1}{2},0,\\delta)\\)-barely robust learnable. However, the definition of a barely robust learner typically implies a probabilistic guarantee over sample choices, not necessarily an expected value over internal random choices. This needs clarification to ensure consistency.

### Suggestions for Improvement

*   **Clarify Corollary 2's Strength:** Please clarify in what sense Corollary 2 provides a stronger boosting guarantee compared to Theorem 1. Specifically, elaborate on whether this strength relates to better sample complexity, weaker requirements on the barely robust learner, or other significant aspects. This would help readers fully appreciate the implications of Corollary 2.

### Conclusion

Despite the noted weaknesses regarding computational efficiency and a minor point in the proof of Theorem 6, the paper's core theoretical contributions are exceptionally strong and highly impactful. The establishment of the equivalence between different robustness notions, the introduction of \\(\\beta\\)-RoBoost, and the clear delineation of robustness requirements offer a significant advancement in the field. The identified weaknesses primarily point to current open problems in adversarial robustness research rather than fundamental flaws in the paper's theoretical framework. The paper is well-written, theoretically sound, and opens up new avenues for research, warranting a strong accept recommendation.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper mainly shows that strongly robust learnable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ is equivalent to barely robust learable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\mathcal{U})$, bu giving an algorithm $\beta$-RoBoost to boost robustness of barely robust learners and an algorithm to return a barely robust classifier through calling the strongly robust learner.

This paper also shows that barely robust learable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ is not sufficient for strongly robust learnable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ by a counterexample. What's more, the paper also gives algorithms to boost robustness with unlabeled data.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- This paper shows a sufficient and necessary condition for strongly robust learning and gives a novelty method to boost robustness of barely robust learners. The theoretical results are quite solid.
- The results of this paper in significant since it provides another way to study the robustness.
- The paper gives some high-level strategies to help understanding the spirit in the algorithms and proofs.

Weaknesses:
- As discussed in the discussion part, verifying whether a classifier is robust on the perturbation set $\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\mathcal{U})$ is a key issue in the computational efficiency of the proposed algorithm, but **to the best of my knowledge, there is now no theoretiacl gurantee for precisely verifying robustness for a general perturbation set and a general hypothesis class**. As stated in [1], the popular method random smooth might fail in verifying the $\ell_\infty$ robustness for high-dimensional images. So the realizability of the proposed algorithm highly depends on the development of robust verification.

[1] Blum, Avrim, et al. "Random Smoothing Might be Unable to Certify $\ell_\infty$ Robustness for High-Dimensional Images." J. Mach. Learn. Res. 21 (2020): 211-1.

Questions:
In the proof of Theorem 6, the paper uses a randomized learner which do not need any data as input, and proves that it is $(\frac{1}{2},0,\delta)$-barely robust learnable. And the paper proves that the expected value of the **robust risk** w.r.t. the random choice of $\tilde{y}$ is $\frac{1}{2}$, but not to be $\frac{1}{2}$ with probability at least $1 - \delta$ over the choice of samples as in the definition of the barely robust learner. Although we can use concentration inequality to get a confidence, but it is over the random choice of $\tilde{y}$, so there seems to be a mismatch in the definition of the barely robust learner and the proof of Theorem 6.

In Corollary 2, the paper claims it is a stronger boosting guarantee, I wonder in what sense is it stronger than Thoerem 1? Is it falls on better sample complexity, or falls on weaker requirement on the barely robust learner, or others else?

Limitations:
The authors does.

reviews.summary: The authors introduce a notion of \emph{barely robust} learning. Their main results demonstrate the an equivalence with strongly robust learning by showing (Theorem 4) that if a concept is strongly robustly learnable, it is barely robustly learnable as well, and then by proposing an oracle-efficient algorithm (Theorem 1) that calls a barely robust learner in order to produce a strongly robust one. Their formal equivalence statement (Corollary I) shows that being strongly robustly learnable with respect to an adversarial perturbation set $\mathcal{U}$ is equivalent to being barely robustly learnable with respect to a larger perturbation set $\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\mathcal{U})$, and they further demonstrate (Theorem 6) that barely robust learnability w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ does not in general imply strong robust learnability.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Originality:
Highly original; the authors have proposed the first algorithm that boosts the robustness of what they call barely robust learners, and provide a comprehensive characterization of their newly proposed notion as it relates to previously studied notions.

Quality:
Good. The theory is elegantly laid out, and comprehensively addresses the salient questions. The discussion provides a satisfying contextualization of the results. 

Clarity:
Generally quite good, although perhaps it would read more straightforwardly if relevant definitions were placed before they are required in theorem statements. (I believe there is a typo in line 151: a missing subscript t in the def. of $G_{h_t}(z)$.)  

Significance: 
The open question of whether one can boost the error of a barely robust learner without sacrificing robustness remains, it would seem, an impediment to greater significance. In the same vein, more insight into the possibility of currently known adversarial learning methods being barely robust would also be welcome.
reviews.questions: See comments under Significance above. Any enlightenment on either of these aspects would greatly improve the paper.
reviews.limitations: The authors have not discussed potential negative societal impact of their work, although it is hard to see what one might have to say here that is not totally banal.

Their formal equivalence statement (Corollary I) shows that being strongly robustly learnable with respect to an adversarial perturbation set $\mathcal{U}$ is equivalent to being barely robustly learnable with respect to a larger perturbation set $\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\mathcal{U})$, and they further demonstrate (Theorem 6) that barely robust learnability w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ does not in general imply strong robust learnability.

Strengths And Weaknesses:

Highly original; the authors have proposed the first algorithm that boosts the robustness of what they call barely robust learners, and provide a comprehensive characterization of their newly proposed notion as it relates to previously studied notions.

The open question of whether one can boost the error of a barely robust learner without sacrificing robustness remains, it would seem, an impediment to greater significance.
