title: Robust Binary Models by Pruning Randomly-initialized Networks

reviews.summary: This paper presents a method for searching binary randomly initialized networks to generate pruned and robust models. The paper leverages the existing edge-popup algorithm for pruning untrained networks via learning important scores instead of model weights. The paper also proposes adding a last layer batchnorm to stabilize the training of their compressed robust models. The paper conducts several experiments to support their claim of achieving state-of-the-art robust binary networks.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The paper is well motivated
- The quality of writing is good, the ideas are well explained
- The experimental results support the authors' claims, achieving state-of-the-art results for pruned binary robust models

Weaknesses:
- The novelty of this work is a bit limited, as it combines existing techniques (edge-popup + adversarial loss similar to [1] and [2]), that being said the end results are very impressive
- I would have liked to see more analysis on why the method is so successful, as opposed to adversarial training and/or binarization/pruning schemes


[1] Ramanujan, V., Wortsman, M., Kembhavi, A., Farhadi, A., & Rastegari, M. (2020). What's hidden in a randomly weighted neural network?. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (pp. 11893-11902).

[2] Sehwag, V., Wang, S., Mittal, P., & Jana, S. (2020). Hydra: Pruning adversarially robust neural networks. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33, 19655-19666.
reviews.questions:
1) In the Signed Kaiming Constant initialization (SKC) in line 175, is the 1-r a typo? since the original paper does not have the pruning ratio in the initialization step. If it is not a typo, does that mean every pruning ratio requires a different randomly initialized network?

2) Is the last normalization layer a standard batchnorm layer, or a homogenous (affine=False) batchnorm layer as used in other layers?

3) The observation that more pruning leads to higher robustness is very intriguing, especially when existing pruning + adversarial training methods, such as HYDRA [1] and ADMM [2], typically show that robustness decreases with the pruning rate (as is expected). Moreover, the edge-pop up algorithm typically achieves its best performance (in the non adversarial setting) when r=0.5, as it maximizes the number of possible network choices. Could the authors shed more light on the reason why their method seems to contradict both these existing observations?

4) If I understood correctly, the proposed method is able to generate 99% pruned binary networks, that achieve adversarial robustness that is higher than all other baselines, which includes full precision adversarially trained models and their pruned versions, as well as other trained binary models. For context, the state-of-the-art 99% pruned full precision baseline achieved by HYDRA suffers massive degradation in terms of robustness, when compared to an unpruned baseline. The results reported in this paper are very impressive and also very intriguing, as they seems too good to be true. I believe more experiments/analysis is warranted to explain why this method is so much more effective than prior work. One simple experiment perhaps is to initialize the weights to constant values between [-1,1] instead of the binary {-1,1}, and keep everything else the same. One would expect that pruning this baseline should have even better robust accuracy than the binarized version, and thus surpass that of any FP baseline. Another question is what would happen if we train the pruned models from scratch (that is take the pruning mask and initialize another model for adversarial training). It would be great if the authors can address this. 

[1] Sehwag, V., Wang, S., Mittal, P., & Jana, S. (2020). Hydra: Pruning adversarially robust neural networks. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33, 19655-19666.

[2] Ye, S., Xu, K., Liu, S., Cheng, H., Lambrechts, J. H., Zhang, H., ... & Lin, X. (2019). Adversarial robustness vs. model compression, or both?. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (pp. 111-120).
reviews.limitations: The authors claim that their work is generic and does not suffer from limitations or potential negative societal impact.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a method for searching binary randomly initialized networks to generate pruned and robust models. The paper leverages the existing edge-popup algorithm for pruning untrained networks via learning important scores instead of model weights. The paper also proposes adding a last layer batchnorm to stabilize the training of their compressed robust models. The paper conducts several experiments to support their claim of achieving state-of-the-art robust binary networks.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The paper is well motivated
- The quality of writing is good, the ideas are well explained
- The experimental results support the authors' claims, achieving state-of-the-art results for pruned binary robust models

Weaknesses:
- The novelty of this work is a bit limited, as it combines existing techniques (edge-popup + adversarial loss similar to [1] and [2]), that being said the end results are very impressive
- I would have liked to see more analysis on why the method is so successful, as opposed to adversarial training and/or binarization/pruning schemes


[1] Ramanujan, V., Wortsman, M., Kembhavi, A., Farhadi, A., & Rastegari, M. (2020). What's hidden in a randomly weighted neural network?. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (pp. 11893-11902).

[2] Sehwag, V., Wang, S., Mittal, P., & Jana, S. (2020). Hydra: Pruning adversarially robust neural networks. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33, 19655-19666.

Questions:
1) In the Signed Kaiming Constant initialization (SKC) in line 175, is the 1-r a typo? since the original paper does not have the pruning ratio in the initialization step. If it is not a typo, does that mean every pruning ratio requires a different randomly initialized network?

2) Is the last normalization layer a standard batchnorm layer, or a homogenous (affine=False) batchnorm layer as used in other layers?

3) The observation that more pruning leads to higher robustness is very intriguing, especially when existing pruning + adversarial training methods, such as HYDRA [1] and ADMM [2], typically show that robustness decreases with the pruning rate (as is expected). Moreover, the edge-pop up algorithm typically achieves its best performance (in the non adversarial setting) when r=0.5, as it maximizes the number of possible network choices. Could the authors shed more light on the reason why their method seems to contradict both these existing observations?

4) If I understood correctly, the proposed method is able to generate 99% pruned binary networks, that achieve adversarial robustness that is higher than all other baselines, which includes full precision adversarially trained models and their pruned versions, as well as other trained binary models. For context, the state-of-the-art 99% pruned full precision baseline achieved by HYDRA suffers massive degradation in terms of robustness, when compared to an unpruned baseline. The results reported in this paper are very impressive and also very intriguing, as they seems too good to be true. I believe more experiments/analysis is warranted to explain why this method is so much more effective than prior work. One simple experiment perhaps is to initialize the weights to constant values between [-1,1] instead of the binary {-1,1}, and keep everything else the same. One would expect that pruning this baseline should have even better robust accuracy than the binarized version, and thus surpass that of any FP baseline. Another question is what would happen if we train the pruned models from scratch (that is take the pruning mask and initialize another model for adversarial training). It would be great if the authors can address this. 

[1] Sehwag, V., Wang, S., Mittal, P., & Jana, S. (2020). Hydra: Pruning adversarially robust neural networks. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33, 19655-19666.

[2] Ye, S., Xu, K., Liu, S., Cheng, H., Lambrechts, J. H., Zhang, H., ... & Lin, X. (2019). Adversarial robustness vs. model compression, or both?. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (pp. 111-120).

Limitations:
The authors claim that their work is generic and does not suffer from limitations or potential negative societal impact.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

This paper introduces an innovative method for obtaining pruned and robust binary models by searching for robust subnetworks within randomly initialized binary networks. The approach leverages the edge-popup algorithm and proposes a novel last batch normalization layer to stabilize the training of these compressed robust models. The authors present compelling experimental evidence to support their claim of achieving state-of-the-art robust accuracy for pruned binary networks, even outperforming some full-precision baselines.

### Strengths

The work is exceptionally well-motivated, addressing a crucial challenge in deploying efficient and secure machine learning models in resource-constrained environments. The paper is well-written, with ideas explained thoroughly and clearly, making it accessible to a broad audience. Crucially, the experimental results are robust and unequivocally support the authors' claims, demonstrating impressive state-of-the-art performance for pruned binary robust models. The achievement of higher adversarial robustness with 99% pruned binary networks compared to full-precision and other binary baselines is particularly noteworthy.

### Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement

While the impressive results are a significant strength, the novelty of the work is somewhat limited, as it primarily combines existing techniques (edge-popup algorithm with adversarial loss).

A more substantial area for improvement lies in providing a more extensive analysis of *why* the method is so remarkably successful. The current paper lacks a deep dive into the mechanisms that enable such superior performance compared to standard adversarial training or other binarization/pruning schemes. Specifically, the observed phenomenon that higher pruning rates lead to higher robustness contradicts findings in existing literature on pruning and adversarial training (e.g., HYDRA, ADMM), where robustness typically decreases with pruning. Similarly, the edge-popup algorithm generally achieves its best performance (in non-adversarial settings) when the pruning ratio `r=0.5`, which contrasts with the higher optimal pruning rates found in this work for robustness. These counter-intuitive findings, especially the "too good to be true" results where highly pruned binary networks achieve higher adversarial robustness than full-precision models, warrant a much more thorough investigation and explanation.

### Specific Suggestions for Improvement

To enhance the paper's impact and address the analytical gaps, I offer the following suggestions:

1.  **Enhanced Analysis**: Provide a more in-depth analysis of the method's effectiveness, particularly explaining the underlying reasons for its significant success when compared to established adversarial training methods and other binarization/pruning techniques. This should include a detailed discussion of the seemingly contradictory findings regarding pruning rates and robustness.
2.  **Further Experiments/Clarifications**: Conduct additional experiments or analyses to explain why this method is significantly more effective than prior work, especially given the surprising results. This could involve probing the specific interactions between edge-popup, binarization, and adversarial training.
3.  **Alternative Initialization**: Experiment with initializing weights to constant values between [-1,1] instead of strictly binary {-1,1}. This might potentially lead to even better robust accuracy and offer further insights.
4.  **Training Pruned Models from Scratch**: Investigate what happens if the pruning masks identified by the method are used to initialize and train another model from scratch using adversarial training. This could help disentangle the benefits of the search process versus the resulting sparse architecture.
5.  **Technical Clarifications**:
    *   Clarify whether '1-r' in the Signed Kaiming Constant initialization (line 175) is a typo and confirm if each pruning ratio requires a different randomly initialized network.
    *   Specify whether the last normalization layer is a standard batch normalization or a homogeneous (affine=False) batch normalization layer.

### Overall Assessment

Despite the need for further analytical depth and clarification on certain technical aspects, this paper presents a technically solid contribution with high potential impact. The well-motivated approach, clear writing, and strong experimental results demonstrating state-of-the-art performance for robust binary networks make this a valuable piece of work. The identified weaknesses are primarily related to providing a deeper understanding and explanation of the observed phenomena, which can be addressed through revision.

**Recommendation:** Accept

Highlighted that the edge-popup algorithm typically achieves best performance (in non-adversarial settings) when r=0.5, contrasting with the paper's findings of higher optimal pruning rates for robustness.
Expressed skepticism about the reported results (99% pruned binary networks achieving higher adversarial robustness than full-precision and other binary baselines) as being 'too good to be true'.

reviews.text:
## New Peer Review

**Overall Assessment:**

This paper presents a compelling and novel approach for training robust binary neural networks from random initialization. The method intelligently combines an adaptive pruning strategy with an edge pop-up algorithm and a PGD-like approach to identify robust binary masks. The empirical results demonstrate significant improvements over existing adversarial training methods for binary models, making a strong case for its practical utility. Given its technical soundness, impressive experimental validation, and clear methodology, I recommend this paper for acceptance.

**Strengths:**

The paper offers several notable strengths that contribute significantly to its impact. Firstly, the proposed training algorithm is exceptionally well-articulated and logically sound, making it easy for readers to understand and potentially implement. Secondly, the experimental results are genuinely impressive, showcasing clear and substantial performance gains compared to current state-of-the-art robust binary network training methods. This robust empirical evidence strongly supports the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed approach. Furthermore, the inclusion of some theoretical analysis for the adaptive pruning method adds valuable depth and rigor to the paper. Finally, the ablation studies and sensitivity analyses, particularly concerning the hyper-parameter 'p' in adaptive pruning, are remarkably clear, well-executed, and consistently align with intuitive expectations, providing excellent insight into the method's components and their contributions.

**Weaknesses:**

While the paper is strong overall, a minor limitation identified is that the experimental evaluation, from the reviewer's perspective at the time of review, primarily focused on a single neural network architecture, ResNet34. While the results on ResNet34 are thorough and convincing, exploring the method's performance across a broader range of architectures could further solidify its generalizability.

**Suggestions for Improvement and Future Work:**

To enhance the paper further, I have a couple of suggestions. Firstly, providing additional intuition or a deeper analytical discussion on *why* this specific training scheme, combining adaptive pruning with edge pop-up and a PGD-like method, proves so effective in achieving robust binary models would be beneficial. This could offer readers a more profound understanding beyond the empirical validation. Secondly, as a direction for future research, it would be highly interesting to investigate whether the proposed training method can be successfully applied to training other critical architectures, such as binary Transformers. This could open up new avenues for robust model development in different domains.

**Conclusion:**

In summary, this paper introduces a technically solid and empirically strong method for robust binary network training. The clarity of the algorithm, the compelling experimental results, and the insightful analyses make it a valuable contribution to the field. The identified weakness is minor, and the suggestions are primarily for further enrichment or future exploration. I therefore recommend its acceptance.

Summary:
This manuscript extends the Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis to binary networks and proposed a new method to obtain compact binary networks with better robustness. The proposed method consists of three main parts: a) adaptively pruning different network layers; b) an effective binary initialization scheme; c) adding the last batch normalization layer. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet-100 validate the effectiveness of the method.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
#### Strength:

1. The paper is well written with sufficient details of the proposed methods.
2. Experimental results on three datasets show superior performance against multiple baseline methods, including vanilla AT, Flying Bird, BCS, RST, HYDRA, and ATMC.
3. Analysis of the derived sparse patterns is provided.

#### Weakness:

1. The proposed methods search for sparse binary subnetworks within randomly-initialized networks. It's unclear whether such a scheme is better than training the parameters directly. It seems the proposed methods (adaptive pruning, binary initialization, and last batch normalization layer) can also to used to optimize the parameters of randomly-initialized networks. 
2. As the proposed methods aim to obtain binary neural networks, which are both compact and robust. The comparison about training and inference cost (e.g., FLOPs) is important.
3. It seems better to firstly report the comparison results in Section 4.2, and then conduct the ablation study of three proposed methods (Section 4.1).

Limitations:
To some extent, this paper may help to build more secure and compact deep learning systems in the real world. But the real-world system faces all types of attacks, which are far beyond adversarial examples.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

**Decision:** 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

This manuscript presents a novel and technically sound extension of the Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis (SLTH) to the domain of robust binary networks. The authors propose an innovative method to derive compact and robust binary models by focusing on finding a robust subnetwork structure within randomly-initialized networks, rather than directly updating parameters. Key components of this approach include an adaptive pruning strategy, an effective binary initialization scheme, and the strategic incorporation of a last batch normalization (LBN) layer to enhance stability and performance.

**Strengths:**

The paper is well-written, providing sufficient technical detail on the proposed methodologies, which aids in understanding and reproducibility. A significant strength lies in the extensive experimental validation. The authors conduct comprehensive experiments across three datasets (CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet-100), demonstrating superior performance in terms of robustness and compactness against a range of state-of-the-art baseline methods, including vanilla Adversarial Training (AT), Flying Bird, BCS, RST, HYDRA, and ATMC. Furthermore, the paper includes an insightful analysis of the derived sparse patterns within the pruned subnetworks, contributing to a deeper understanding of the method's efficacy.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:**

While the paper makes a valuable contribution, there are several areas where it could be significantly strengthened:

1.  **Comparison of Subnetwork Search vs. Direct Training:** The core technical approach involves pruning randomly-initialized binary networks to find a robust subnetwork structure. It remains unclear whether this scheme of searching for sparse binary subnetworks within randomly-initialized networks offers a superior advantage compared to directly training the parameters of a binary network. The proposed components (adaptive pruning, binary initialization, and the LBN layer) could potentially also be applied within a direct parameter optimization framework.
    *   **Suggestion:** The authors should either clarify the theoretical or empirical advantages of their subnetwork search scheme over direct parameter training, or include a comparative study to empirically demonstrate its efficacy.
2.  **Analysis of Training and Inference Costs:** For compact and robust models, a crucial aspect is their efficiency. The paper currently lacks a comparison of training and inference costs, such as FLOPs, for the proposed binary neural networks. This omission makes it difficult to fully appreciate the efficiency benefits of the approach.
    *   **Suggestion:** Please include a comprehensive comparison of training and inference costs (e.g., FLOPs) against the baselines to highlight the compactness and efficiency benefits of the proposed method.
3.  **Organizational Flow of Experimental Results:** The current organization of the experimental results presents the ablation study (Section 4.1) before the main comparison results (Section 4.2). This order can make it challenging for readers to grasp the full performance context before delving into the individual component analyses.
    *   **Suggestion:** Reorder the experimental sections to first present the overall comparison results (Section 4.2), followed by the ablation study (Section 4.1). This would improve the logical flow and readability of the experimental section.

**Technical Details and Further Discussion:**

The method introduces an adaptive pruning strategy that determines layer-wise parameter retention based on a hyper-parameter 'p', allowing for a balance between fixed pruning rate and fixed number of parameters. The binary initialization scheme for model parameters (+1 or -1) and scores (uniform distribution) is well-defined. The introduction of the last batch normalization layer effectively addresses numerical instability caused by large output logits from binary initialization, improving optimization and robustness to score initialization. The experimental evaluation uses PGD and AutoAttack (AA) for robustness metrics, and compares against baselines like FlyingBird, BCS, RST, HYDRA, ATMC, and adversarial training (AT), providing a solid benchmark. The authors acknowledge a limitation regarding the scope of attacks considered, noting that real-world systems face a broader range of attacks beyond adversarial examples, which is a fair point for future work.

**Conclusion:**

This paper presents a strong contribution by effectively extending the Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis to robust binary networks, introducing a novel method with demonstrated superior performance against several state-of-the-art baselines. The proposed adaptive pruning, binary initialization, and LBN layer are well-conceived components. While the paper has several notable strengths, addressing the identified weaknesses regarding the comparative advantage of the subnetwork search scheme, the lack of cost analysis, and the organization of experimental results would significantly enhance its impact and clarity. Given the innovative approach and promising experimental results, I recommend a "Borderline accept" for this paper, contingent on the authors thoroughly addressing the highlighted suggestions for improvement in their revision.

Summary:
This manuscript extends the Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis to binary networks and proposed a new method to obtain compact binary networks with better robustness. The proposed method consists of three main parts: a) adaptively pruning different network layers; b) an effective binary initialization scheme; c) adding the last batch normalization layer. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet-100 validate the effectiveness of the method.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
#### Strength:

1. The paper is well written with sufficient details of the proposed methods.
2. Experimental results on three datasets show superior performance against multiple baseline methods, including vanilla AT, Flying Bird, BCS, RST, HYDRA, and ATMC.
3. Analysis of the derived sparse patterns is provided.

#### Weakness:

1. The proposed methods search for sparse binary subnetworks within randomly-initialized networks. It's unclear whether such a scheme is better than training the parameters directly. It seems the proposed methods (adaptive pruning, binary initialization, and last batch normalization layer) can also to used to optimize the parameters of randomly-initialized networks. 
2. As the proposed methods aim to obtain binary neural networks, which are both compact and robust. The comparison about training and inference cost (e.g., FLOPs) is important.
3. It seems better to firstly report the comparison results in Section 4.2, and then conduct the ablation study of three proposed methods (Section 4.1).

Limitations:
To some extent, this paper may help to build more secure and compact deep learning systems in the real world. But the real-world system faces all types of attacks, which are far beyond adversarial examples.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary: For a robust binary model built with Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis, the paper proposes a model compress method with three strategies: adaptive layer-wise pruning, a binary initialization scheme, and adding a last batch normalization layer. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 show that the proposed method outperforms previous competitive methods such as ATMC [22].
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths.
1) In Section 1 and Section 3.1 (Equation (2)), the paper clearly introduces the base model [19] that the paper works on: a robust binary model built with Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis.
2) In Section 3.2 and Section 3.2, introduction of the proposed three techniques is easy-to-follow, except too more "In Appendix ...". The proposed three techniques, adaptive layer-wise pruning, a binary initialization scheme, and adding a last batch normalization layer, are reasonable.
3) Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 show that the proposed method outperforms previous competitive methods such as ATMC [22]. The paper further provides necessary analysis on the subnetwork patterns, and sensitivity analysis on the pruning strategy and added last batch-normalization layer.

Weaknesses.
1) My major concern is that the paper's proposed three techniques are not systematical but auxiliary model compression strategies. For example, the proposed adaptive pruning is a straightforward trade-off between fixed pruning rate and fixed number of parameters, and the key parameter $p$ is manually but not automatically selected in all experiments. Moreover, the paper works on a base model [19] which is already a robust binary model. The paper focuses on model compression only but not further improving binarization and adversarial robustness. 
2) In Table 4, different methods use different $p$, e.g., $p=1.0$ for RST and $p=0.1$ for the proposed method. I think all methods should use the same $p$ value for fair comparison.
3) Since tiny-scale datasets CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 are easily to be overfitting. I suggest the authors use large-scale datasets such as tinyImageNet for experiments.
reviews.questions:
1) Are the proposed three techniques useful for regular DNNs in addition to the robust binary models [19]?
2) Why do the methods in Table 4 use different $p$ value? Are the methods in Table 4 fairly compared with each other?
3) Is the proposed method scalable to large-scale datasets such as tinyImageNet?
reviews.limitations: As mentioned above, a major limitation is that the paper's proposed three techniques are not systematical but auxiliary model compression strategies.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
For a robust binary model built with Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis, the paper proposes a model compress method with three strategies: adaptive layer-wise pruning, a binary initialization scheme, and adding a last batch normalization layer. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 show that the proposed method outperforms previous competitive methods such as ATMC [22].

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths.
1) In Section 1 and Section 3.1 (Equation (2)), the paper clearly introduces the base model [19] that the paper works on: a robust binary model built with Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis.
2) In Section 3.2 and Section 3.2, introduction of the proposed three techniques is easy-to-follow, except too more "In Appendix ...". The proposed three techniques, adaptive layer-wise pruning, a binary initialization scheme, and adding a last batch normalization layer, are reasonable.
3) Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 show that the proposed method outperforms previous competitive methods such as ATMC [22]. The paper further provides necessary analysis on the subnetwork patterns, and sensitivity analysis on the pruning strategy and added last batch-normalization layer.

Weaknesses.
1) My major concern is that the paper's proposed three techniques are not systematical but auxiliary model compression strategies. For example, the proposed adaptive pruning is a straightforward trade-off between fixed pruning rate and fixed number of parameters, and the key parameter $p$ is manually but not automatically selected in all experiments. Moreover, the paper works on a base model [19] which is already a robust binary model. The paper focuses on model compression only but not further improving binarization and adversarial robustness. 
2) In Table 4, different methods use different $p$, e.g., $p=1.0$ for RST and $p=0.1$ for the proposed method. I think all methods should use the same $p$ value for fair comparison.
3) Since tiny-scale datasets CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 are easily to be overfitting. I suggest the authors use large-scale datasets such as tinyImageNet for experiments.

Questions:
1) Are the proposed three techniques useful for regular DNNs in addition to the robust binary models [19]?
2) Why do the methods in Table 4 use different $p$ value? Are the methods in Table 4 fairly compared with each other?
3) Is the proposed method scalable to large-scale datasets such as tinyImageNet?

Limitations:
As mentioned above, a major limitation is that the paper's proposed three techniques are not systematical but auxiliary model compression strategies.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
This paper introduces a model compression method specifically designed for robust binary models, which are constructed using the Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis (as referenced by [19]). The proposed approach integrates three key techniques: an adaptive layer-wise pruning strategy, a novel binary initialization scheme, and the inclusion of a last batch normalization (LBN) layer. The authors present experimental results on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets, claiming that their method outperforms existing competitive approaches, such as ATMC (referenced by [22]).

**Strengths:**

The paper is well-structured and clear in its presentation. The foundational robust binary model is clearly introduced, providing necessary context for the proposed work. The three novel techniques are described in an easy-to-follow manner, and their integration appears reasonable within the context of model compression. Furthermore, the empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets suggests that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared to certain competitive baselines. The authors also provide valuable analyses, including insights into subnetwork patterns and sensitivity analyses for both the pruning strategy and the LBN layer, which contribute to understanding the method's behavior.

**Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement:**

While the paper presents interesting techniques, several significant concerns lead to a borderline reject recommendation.

1.  **Nature of Proposed Techniques:** The three proposed techniques, while useful, are largely presented as auxiliary strategies for model compression rather than fundamental, systematic advancements in the field. Their contribution feels incremental rather than a significant paradigm shift.
    *   **Suggestion:** It would be beneficial to investigate whether these proposed techniques, particularly the adaptive pruning and binary initialization, could offer similar benefits when applied to regular deep neural networks (DNNs) beyond the scope of robust binary models. This could broaden the perceived impact and generality of the work.

2.  **Manual Parameter Selection for Adaptive Pruning:** A major weakness lies in the adaptive pruning strategy, which, despite its name, relies on a manually selected key parameter 'p' across all experiments. This parameter directly controls the trade-off between a fixed pruning rate and a fixed number of parameters, and its manual selection limits the strategy's autonomy and generalizability.
    *   **Suggestion:** The authors should explore methods to automate the selection of the parameter 'p' for adaptive pruning. This would significantly enhance the robustness and practical applicability of the proposed method.

3.  **Fairness of Experimental Comparisons:** The comparison presented in Table 4 raises concerns regarding fairness. It appears that different methods are evaluated using disparate 'p' values (e.g., p=1.0 for RST versus p=0.1 for the proposed method). Such discrepancies can skew results and make direct comparisons misleading.
    *   **Suggestion:** To ensure a fair and rigorous comparison, it is imperative that all methods in Table 4, and indeed throughout the experimental section, are evaluated under identical conditions, specifically using the same 'p' value where applicable.

4.  **Limited Scope and Focus:** The paper's scope is quite narrow, focusing exclusively on model compression for an *already robust* binary model. It does not delve into further improving the fundamental aspects of binarization or adversarial robustness itself. The techniques are applied to an existing robust model without addressing deeper challenges in these areas.
    *   **Suggestion:** Future work could explore how these compression techniques might be integrated with efforts to directly enhance binarization processes or improve adversarial robustness beyond just compressing an existing robust model.

5.  **Dataset Limitations:** The reliance on tiny-scale datasets such as CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 for all experiments is a significant limitation. Results obtained on these datasets are often susceptible to overfitting and may not generalize well to more complex, real-world scenarios.
    *   **Suggestion:** To bolster the credibility and generalizability of the findings, experiments should be extended to larger-scale datasets, such as tinyImageNet. This would provide a more robust evaluation of the proposed method's performance under more challenging conditions.

**Main Points Summarized:**

In summary, while the paper offers some interesting auxiliary model compression strategies and demonstrates empirical gains, the core issues revolve around the manual selection of the adaptive pruning parameter 'p', which limits generality; the perceived unfairness in experimental comparisons due to differing 'p' values; the use of small datasets prone to overfitting; and the narrow scope, which focuses solely on compressing existing robust binary models without addressing broader improvements in binarization or adversarial robustness. These limitations collectively detract from the overall impact and reliability of the presented work.

**Recommendation:**

**4: Borderline reject:** While the paper introduces clearly presented and reasonable techniques for model compression in robust binary networks, and shows promising results on smaller datasets, the identified weaknesses are substantial. The reliance on manually tuned parameters, the potential for unfair comparisons, and the limited scope and scale of evaluation significantly outweigh the strengths. Addressing these concerns, particularly regarding the fairness of comparisons and the robustness of evaluation on larger datasets, would greatly strengthen the paper.

Strengths.
1) In Section 1 and Section 3.1 (Equation (2)), the paper clearly introduces the base model [19] that the paper works on: a robust binary model built with Strong Lottery Ticket Hypothesis.
2) In Section 3.2 and Section 3.2, introduction of the proposed three techniques is easy-to-follow, except too more "In Appendix ...". The proposed three techniques, adaptive layer-wise pruning, a binary initialization scheme, and adding a last batch normalization layer, are reasonable.
3) Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 show that the proposed method outperforms previous competitive methods such as ATMC [22]. The paper further provides necessary analysis on the subnetwork patterns, and sensitivity analysis on the pruning strategy and added last batch-normalization layer.

Weaknesses.
1) My major concern is that the paper's proposed three techniques are not systematical but auxiliary model compression strategies. For example, the proposed adaptive pruning is a straightforward trade-off between fixed pruning rate and fixed number of parameters, and the key parameter $p$ is manually but not automatically selected in all experiments. Moreover, the paper works on a base model [19] which is already a robust binary model. The paper focuses on model compression only but not further improving binarization and adversarial robustness. 
2) In Table 4, different methods use different $p$, e.g., $p=1.0$ for RST and $p=0.1$ for the proposed method. I think all methods should use the same $p$ value for fair comparison.
3) Since tiny-scale datasets CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 are easily to be overfitting. I suggest the authors use large-scale datasets such as tinyImageNet for experiments.

Questions:
1) Are the proposed three techniques useful for regular DNNs in addition to the robust binary models [19]?
2) Why do the methods in Table 4 use different $p$ value? Are the methods in Table 4 fairly compared with each other?
3) Is the proposed method scalable to large-scale datasets such as tinyImageNet?

Limitations:
As mentioned above, a major limitation is that the paper's proposed three techniques are not systematical but auxiliary model compression strategies.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review

This paper introduces EGRU, a novel event-based Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) specifically designed to facilitate activity-sparse inference and learning. The core innovation lies in EGRU's ability to activate only a subset of its units at any given time, thereby promising a more efficient computational paradigm. The authors convincingly argue that this approach significantly reduces the computational load, enhancing both energy efficiency and scalability for recurrent neural networks.

**Strengths:**

The proposed EGRU model represents a genuinely novel and original contribution to the field of efficient neural network architectures. The technical formulation of EGRU is sound, with its claims rigorously supported by both theoretical analysis and comprehensive experimental validation. The paper demonstrates a thorough understanding of the existing literature, providing a comprehensive and well-discussed overview of related works, which helps contextualize EGRU's advancements.

A significant strength of this work is the extensive experimental evaluation. The authors have conducted experiments across a diverse range of task types, thoroughly discussing and comparing results in terms of critical metrics such as accuracy, Multiply-Accumulate operations (#MAC), and sparsity. This detailed analysis provides strong empirical evidence for EGRU's effectiveness. Furthermore, the paper is exceptionally well-written and organized, making it clear and accessible to readers. The potential for EGRU to achieve greater energy efficiency and scalability through its reduced computational demands during both inference and learning phases is a particularly compelling aspect of this research.

**Weaknesses:**

No major weaknesses were identified in this submission.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

While the paper thoroughly evaluates EGRU's sparsity, it would be beneficial to include a comparative analysis of EGRU's activity sparsity during inference against that achieved by pruning a standard GRU. Specifically, a discussion or experimental comparison with methods like those explored in "EXPLORING SPARSITY IN RECURRENT NEURAL NETWORKS" could provide valuable additional context and further highlight the unique advantages or trade-offs of the event-based approach.

**Overall Assessment:**

This paper presents a technically solid and well-executed contribution to the field of efficient neural networks. The proposed EGRU model is novel, theoretically sound, and empirically validated through extensive experiments. The potential for improved energy efficiency and scalability makes this work impactful. While the suggested comparison with pruned GRUs could strengthen the paper further, it does not detract from the current quality of the work.

**Recommendation:**

**6: Weak Accept** - The paper is technically solid, presents a novel and impactful idea, and has no major concerns regarding evaluation, resources, reproducibility, or ethical considerations. The minor suggestion does not necessitate a major revision.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review

**Overall Assessment:**

The paper introduces EGRU, an event-based continuous-time GRU variant, alongside an innovative event-based backpropagation training method. The authors present experiments demonstrating competitive performance against state-of-the-art recurrent models and highlight the high activity-sparsity achieved during both training and inference. The core approach is genuinely novel, and the paper is well-written, making a valuable contribution to the field of event-based neural networks.

**Strengths:**

*   **Novel Approach:** The proposed EGRU architecture and its event-based backpropagation training method are novel, offering a fresh perspective on continuous-time recurrent neural networks and their application in event-driven contexts.
*   **Well-Written Paper:** The manuscript is clearly written and well-structured, making the complex concepts accessible to the reader.
*   **Good Experiments:** The experiments presented are well-designed and effectively demonstrate the competitive performance of EGRU against established recurrent models.

**Weaknesses and Main Points for Improvement:**

While the paper presents a promising and novel approach, several key areas require attention to strengthen its claims, enhance its completeness, and ensure scientific rigor:

*   **Missing Ablation Study:** A significant omission is the absence of an ablation study. Such a study would be crucial for understanding the individual contributions of the different components within the EGRU architecture and their impact on performance and sparsity.
*   **Limited Comparison with Sparse/Scalable RNNs:** The paper lacks a comprehensive comparison with existing sparse and scalable recurrent neural network (RNN) learning methods. Specifically, there is no comparison with sparse Real-Time Recurrent Learning (RTRL) or other recurrent architectures/learning approaches that explicitly address scalability issues. This limits the understanding of EGRU's standing relative to other methods tackling similar challenges.
*   **Insufficient Efficiency Comparison:** While claims of high activity-sparsity are made, the computational efficiency of the current approach needs explicit comparison against other sparse RNNs, particularly those based on Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). The paper does not provide concrete quantitative measures such as FLOPS (Floating Point Operations Per Second) for both training and inference, making it difficult to fully substantiate computational efficiency claims against both sparse and dense RNNs.
*   **Incomplete Experimental Details:**
    *   **Hyper-parameter Optimization:** Insufficient details are provided regarding hyper-parameter optimization, and the rationale for the chosen parameters is missing. This hinders reproducibility and understanding of the experimental setup.
    *   **Lack of Statistical Significance:** The experiments lack reporting of statistical significance. Average performance and standard errors from multiple trials (e.g., K trials) are not presented, which is crucial for robust scientific claims.
    *   **Robustness and Stability:** There is no discussion or experimentation regarding the robustness and stability of the EGRU model across different seeds and settings, especially when compared to dense models trained using BPTT.
*   **Missing Key Citations:** Some key citations relevant to sparse recurrent models and scalable learning methods appear to be missing. Including these would help contextualize the work within the broader literature and acknowledge related advancements.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

To address the identified weaknesses and enhance the paper's impact and rigor, I recommend the following:

*   **Expanded Comparisons:**
    *   Compare EGRU with at least two additional sparse recurrent models from the provided references [1-5], specifically including sparse RTRL and other recurrent architectures/learning approaches that address scalability issues.
    *   Provide a detailed comparison of EGRU's efficiency with other sparse RNNs, especially for BPTT-based models, to contextualize its performance.
*   **Quantitative Efficiency Metrics:** Provide FLOPS numbers for both training and inference for EGRU, and compare them quantitatively with other sparse and dense RNNs. This will concretely substantiate the claims regarding computational efficiency.
*   **Enhanced Experimental Rigor:**
    *   **Hyper-parameter Justification:** Provide clear reasoning for the choice of hyper-parameters used in the experiments.
    *   **Statistical Reporting:** Conduct experiments for K trials (e.g., 3-5) and report average performance along with the standard error to establish statistical significance for the results.
    *   **Robustness Analysis:** Include a discussion or experimental analysis on the robustness and stability of the EGRU model across different seeds and settings, comparing it to dense models trained using BPTT.
*   **Ablation Study:** Incorporate an ablation study to demonstrate the contribution of individual components of the EGRU architecture.
*   **Citations:** Ensure all relevant prior work, particularly concerning sparse and scalable recurrent learning, is appropriately cited to provide a comprehensive literature review.

**Recommendation:**

This paper introduces a novel and well-presented event-based GRU variant with an innovative training method. The demonstrated performance and inherent sparsity are promising, indicating a valuable contribution to the field. While the identified weaknesses regarding experimental rigor, detailed comparisons with existing sparse methods, and efficiency metrics are significant, they appear to be addressable through further focused experimentation and clearer reporting rather than indicating fundamental flaws in the proposed approach. Given the novelty and potential impact of EGRU, I recommend **Weak Accept**. Addressing the suggestions thoroughly would significantly strengthen the paper for publication.

reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes an event-based continuous-time variant of the GRU model, named EGRU, motivated by building scalable, energy-efficient deep recurrent models. The EGRU unit only outputs events when the internal states reach a trainable threshold, which makes it activity-sparsity naturally. Furthermore, the authors derive corresponding BP algorithms for updating EGRU and extend it to a discrete-time version that is easier to implement by popular machine learning architecture. As the experiments show, the EGRU model exhibits comparable performance to state-of-the-art recurrent network architectures for several tasks.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
This paper is well-written, consistent, and produces good results. By introducing the event generating mechanism, the EGRU model not only significantly reduces the multiply-accumulate operations but also preserves the performance that is comparable to the more computation-expensive GRU-based model. 

Strengths:
+ The event generating mechanism is a novel module inserted into the GRU model, which is proved to effectively reduce the computation of the original model.
+ The authors conduct three downstream applications to validate the effectiveness of the EGRU model, which are convincing and with comparable performances to previous methods.

Weakness:
- For the delay-copy task, the authors only show a simple example. How about increasing the number of EGRU units? Besides, did the events generating mechanism improve the memory ability, compared to the GRU model?
- In gesture prediction, the authors down sample frames into 32x32, which may affect the performance. I wonder how the input size affects the prediction accuracy with EGRU model. In general, the larger-resolution images contain more detailed information, which could be helpful for classification. 
- For language modeling experiments, the performance decreases with the increment of EGRU units. Can the authors give some explanation or the trend of performance related to the number of EGRU units?
reviews.questions:
This paper presents a novel GRU architecture, which balances the performance and computational complexity. 
- However, in the downstream application with EGRU, some experiments’ setups are unclear, as listed in Weakness. I expect the authors to clarify these points. 
- Besides, the authors apply EGRU in some special cases. Since the LSTM/GRU are applied widely in CV/NLP applications, can they be replaced by EGRU to reduce the computation complexity in general? 
- As GRU is simplified from LSTM, can event generating mechanism transfer to the LSTM model?
reviews.limitations: The authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work in discussion section.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes an event-based continuous-time variant of the GRU model, named EGRU, motivated by building scalable, energy-efficient deep recurrent models. The EGRU unit only outputs events when the internal states reach a trainable threshold, which makes it activity-sparsity naturally. Furthermore, the authors derive corresponding BP algorithms for updating EGRU and extend it to a discrete-time version that is easier to implement by popular machine learning architecture. As the experiments show, the EGRU model exhibits comparable performance to state-of-the-art recurrent network architectures for several tasks.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
This paper is well-written, consistent, and produces good results. By introducing the event generating mechanism, the EGRU model not only significantly reduces the multiply-accumulate operations but also preserves the performance that is comparable to the more computation-expensive GRU-based model. 

Strengths:
+ The event generating mechanism is a novel module inserted into the GRU model, which is proved to effectively reduce the computation of the original model.
+ The authors conduct three downstream applications to validate the effectiveness of the EGRU model, which are convincing and with comparable performances to previous methods.

Weakness:
- For the delay-copy task, the authors only show a simple example. How about increasing the number of EGRU units? Besides, did the events generating mechanism improve the memory ability, compared to the GRU model?
- In gesture prediction, the authors down sample frames into 32x32, which may affect the performance. I wonder how the input size affects the prediction accuracy with EGRU model. In general, the larger-resolution images contain more detailed information, which could be helpful for classification. 
- For language modeling experiments, the performance decreases with the increment of EGRU units. Can the authors give some explanation or the trend of performance related to the number of EGRU units?

Questions:
This paper presents a novel GRU architecture, which balances the performance and computational complexity. 
- However, in the downstream application with EGRU, some experiments’ setups are unclear, as listed in Weakness. I expect the authors to clarify these points. 
- Besides, the authors apply EGRU in some special cases. Since the LSTM/GRU are applied widely in CV/NLP applications, can they be replaced by EGRU to reduce the computation complexity in general? 
- As GRU is simplified from LSTM, can event generating mechanism transfer to the LSTM model?

Limitations:
The authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work in discussion section.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
This paper introduces EGRU, a novel event-based continuous-time variant of the Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) model, designed to achieve scalable and energy-efficient deep recurrent models through natural activity-sparsity. The core idea is that EGRU units compute updates only upon receiving input events, which are triggered when internal states reach a trainable threshold. The authors have also derived corresponding event-based backpropagation algorithms for the continuous-time EGRU, including a discrete-time simplification for easier implementation with popular machine learning libraries.

Overall, I find the paper to be well-written, consistent, and technically solid. The proposed EGRU model demonstrates significant potential, achieving a substantial reduction in multiply-accumulate (MAC) operations during both inference and training. Crucially, this reduction in computational complexity is achieved while maintaining competitive performance against state-of-the-art recurrent networks across various real-world tasks.

**Strengths:**

*   **Novelty and Efficiency:** The event generating mechanism is a genuinely novel module inserted into the GRU architecture. It effectively introduces activity-sparsity, leading to a significant reduction in computational operations (MACs) without compromising performance, which is a key advantage for scalable and energy-efficient models.
*   **Empirical Validation:** The authors conduct convincing experiments across three diverse downstream applications: a delay-copy task, gesture prediction using the DVS128 Gesture Dataset, and language modeling on the PennTreebank dataset. In these applications, EGRU consistently produces good results, showing performance comparable to more computation-expensive GRU-based models and previous methods.
*   **Technical Soundness:** The paper clearly outlines the EGRU architecture and the derivation of its backpropagation algorithms, demonstrating a solid technical foundation.

**Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement:**

While the paper presents a strong technical contribution and promising results, several aspects of the evaluation and explanation could benefit from deeper analysis:

*   **Delay-Copy Task Evaluation:** For the delay-copy task, the paper only presents a simple example. It would be beneficial to explore the model's behavior with an increased number of EGRU units to better understand its scalability and limits. Furthermore, the paper does not clarify whether the event generating mechanism inherently improves memory ability compared to a standard GRU model for this task, which is a critical aspect for recurrent networks.
*   **Gesture Prediction Input Resolution:** In the gesture prediction experiments, frames are downsampled to 32x32. While this might be a common practice, the paper does not explore how different input sizes (e.g., higher resolution images) might affect prediction accuracy with the EGRU model. This analysis would provide a more complete understanding of its performance characteristics under varying conditions.
*   **Language Modeling Performance Trend:** The language modeling experiments show an interesting trend where performance decreases with an increment of EGRU units. Currently, no explanation for this observed behavior is provided, which leaves a gap in understanding the model's scaling properties for this specific application.
*   **General Applicability and Transferability:** The paper could benefit from a discussion on the general applicability of EGRU to replace existing LSTM/GRU models in common computer vision (CV) and natural language processing (NLP) applications to reduce computational complexity. Additionally, investigating if the event generating mechanism can be effectively transferred to the LSTM model would be a valuable extension, demonstrating broader utility.
*   **Clarity of Experimental Setups:** Some experimental setups in the downstream applications mentioned above could be clarified to ensure full reproducibility and understanding.

**Suggestions for Authors:**

Based on the identified weaknesses, I recommend the following improvements:

1.  For the delay-copy task, investigate and report the behavior with an increased number of EGRU units.
2.  Provide an analysis comparing the memory ability introduced by the event generating mechanism to that of the standard GRU model.
3.  In gesture prediction, analyze how different input sizes (e.g., higher resolution images) affect prediction accuracy with the EGRU model.
4.  Offer an explanation for the observed decrease in language modeling performance as the number of EGRU units increases.
5.  Clarify any ambiguous experimental setups in the downstream applications.
6.  Discuss the general applicability of EGRU as a drop-in replacement for LSTM/GRU in common CV/NLP applications for computational complexity reduction.
7.  Explore the transferability of the event generating mechanism to the LSTM model.

**Overall Assessment:**

This paper presents a technically solid and well-written contribution with the introduction of EGRU, a novel model that effectively reduces computational complexity through activity-sparsity while maintaining competitive performance. The core idea is innovative and well-validated across several tasks. However, the evaluation could be strengthened by a deeper analysis of certain experimental aspects and more comprehensive explanations for observed behaviors. Given the significant strengths and the promising direction of the research, the reasons to accept this paper outweigh the reasons to reject, provided the authors address the identified areas for improvement.

**Rating:** 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes an event-based continuous-time variant of the GRU model, named EGRU, motivated by building scalable, energy-efficient deep recurrent models. The EGRU unit only outputs events when the internal states reach a trainable threshold, which makes it activity-sparsity naturally. Furthermore, the authors derive corresponding BP algorithms for updating EGRU and extend it to a discrete-time version that is easier to implement by popular machine learning architecture. As the experiments show, the EGRU model exhibits comparable performance to state-of-the-art recurrent network architectures for several tasks.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
This paper is well-written, consistent, and produces good results. By introducing the event generating mechanism, the EGRU model not only significantly reduces the multiply-accumulate operations but also preserves the performance that is comparable to the more computation-expensive GRU-based model. 

Strengths:
+ The event generating mechanism is a novel module inserted into the GRU model, which is proved to effectively reduce the computation of the original model.
+ The authors conduct three downstream applications to validate the effectiveness of the EGRU model, which are convincing and with comparable performances to previous methods.

Weakness:
- For the delay-copy task, the authors only show a simple example. How about increasing the number of EGRU units? Besides, did the events generating mechanism improve the memory ability, compared to the GRU model?
- In gesture prediction, the authors down sample frames into 32x32, which may affect the performance. I wonder how the input size affects the prediction accuracy with EGRU model. In general, the larger-resolution images contain more detailed information, which could be helpful for classification. 
- For language modeling experiments, the performance decreases with the increment of EGRU units. Can the authors give some explanation or the trend of performance related to the number of EGRU units?

Questions:
This paper presents a novel GRU architecture, which balances the performance and computational complexity. 
- However, in the downstream application with EGRU, some experiments’ setups are unclear, as listed in Weakness. I expect the authors to clarify these points. 
- Besides, the authors apply EGRU in some special cases. Since the LSTM/GRU are applied widely in CV/NLP applications, can they be replaced by EGRU to reduce the computation complexity in general? 
- As GRU is simplified from LSTM, can event generating mechanism transfer to the LSTM model?

Limitations:
The authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work in discussion section.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
